sentences.text: Prochnik.
OK.
Actually, I'll start with my name [LAUGHS] because I heard you introduce me as 'Proch-nick.' or something like that.
And it's interesting because it's-- the whole past is right there. 'Proh-knik' is the way the name is pronounced.
And I guess it'll never be pronounced like that again.
I knew that when I came to the United States.
But sometimes, when I hear it pronounced correctly-- the way it was pronounced in Poland-- it brings back a lot of memories.
And that sort of starts everything going.
I was born in Cracow, Poland, in May of 1933.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember, obviously, details going back that far, but I have a lot of memories of what, you know, of my childhood between then and the time the war, you know, send us running.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My family was-- we were obviously Jewish.
We-- my mother's side of the family was-- at that point in time, when I was born-- was very well-to-do.
We had a large chocolate factory-- Suchard Chocolate, which still exists probably everywhere in the world.
It was a Swiss company, and we had the Polish franchise.

So I was born into a world of governesses, and cooks, and four or five vacations a year, and, you know, a very beautiful house in Cracow, which I think later served as headquarters for the Gestapo, as a matter of fact.
One of the many ironies of life.
But it was a very privileged life.
And at the same time, my grandfather-- my mother's father, the head of this wealthy family-- was very, very Orthodox man.
And in fact, we had a little shul right in the house downstairs that was used on certain-- for certain holidays.
I don't think it would-- worked-- you know, I mean, it was not used every day or every week, but on special holidays, it functioned as a shul.
Yes, my-- there were four floors to this house, with an elevator, which was a big deal in Poland prior to World War II.
Every one of the floors had two apartments, and on each floor, one of my uncles and aunts and their children-- occupants.
So there were four families directly part of the Lax family that occupied each floor.
We were on the top floor.
And my-- the other four apartments were rented out to other tenants.
I-- when I saw-- I've never been back there, and I've-- I've heard that the house was completely-- it wasn't destroyed because Cracow was an open city.
In other words, it wasn't bombed during World War II, unlike Warsaw.
The house was not destroyed, but I think it was completely re-- you know, changed and remodeled and redone.
So all I had were these childhood memories of me playing in this fairly luxurious and what seemed to me very large apartment that I lived in with my mother, father, and my sister.
And then when I saw there's a scene in the movie where Schindler takes over an apartment that used to belong to wealthy Jews that he's provided with an apartment by the Nazis, you know.
And I said, my God, that's our apartment.
The shape of it-- the shape of the rooms-- that rectangular look of those rooms.
Just absolutely, just it was amazing to see it.
Very nice.
[LAUGHS] No, it was.
It was-- I don't remember the-- the specific, exact, you know, setup of every day.
Here are some of the memories I have.
We had a wonderful cook-- also Polish.
My governess was Polish German, and my mother had specifically hired her because she wanted us to be able to speak German as well as Polish.
My mother's generation, unlike my grandfather, was already the ones where they spoke-- not Yiddish, or very little Yiddish-- they spoke Polish and German equally well.
Because they had lived in Vienna-- in Austria.
They had lived in Poland, et cetera, et cetera.
So this governess was hired for my sister and myself, who spoke German as well she spoke Polish.
And that's how we were raised in those first years of our life.
She was Catholic.
I guess, probably, our cook was too.
So the food-- I remember the food being wonderful.
I mean, you know, because we had this wonderful cook.
And I remember very wonderful things like going off to ski in a place called Zakopane where I think they held the Olympics in the 1930s.
Every winter, we would go skiing there.
And then in the spring, we would-- or in the summer, we would go to Italy to the Italian Riviera.
And then in the fall, my mother would send my sister [LAUGHS] and me up to Czechoslovakia-- to the mountains in Czechoslovakia-- for a couple of weeks so we could get the good air for our lungs.
Czechoslovakian air.
Yes.
The answer is yes.
By memory, is spending more time with the governess.
My mother's role was of, I guess, that upper middle class position that she found herself in.
They hadn't always been like that.
They had been very poor until after World War I.
But that meant that I saw my mother at meal time.
You know, she would come in and say prayers with me in the evening.
It wasn't-- she wasn't distant.
She was there, but she didn't-- she never went in the kitchen and cooked.
She had no idea how you cooked a meal.
But my memory is of spending most of my time with my governess.
And maybe we'll talk about that later, but the loss of my governess was a very central event in my life because she was more like my mother to me than my mother was.
Absolutely, yes.
Kosher home.
All the holidays were celebrated.
There was a succah that was set up, you know, during Succot.
And you know, the little-- you put out-- you put it together.
A little room that is, has just fruit harvest-- fruits of the harvest-- that hung from the ceiling.
That sort of stuff.
Passover.
I sort of remember that.
I don't remember too much of the holidays.
I remember being taken, you know, and being in the temple.
I don't remember going to other synagogues.
So I don't remember what that was like.
What I do remember, interestingly enough, is our governess taking my sister and myself secretly with her to church.
Catholic church in Cracow.
You couldn't get more Catholic than that.
Dark churches with the smell of incense.

And we understood-- I don't know if she asked us that or told us that we understood, obviously, that this was not something to be mentioned in the presence of my parents because that would have given them strokes and she would have been fired on the spot.
So there was that awareness of this other world-- the Catholic world-- which was really a very dominant part of life in Cracow.
Cousins.
Cousins.
Yes, I have cousins.
I had a cousin who was one day younger than I was.
I had two other cousins who were more or less in the age bracket.
Oh, and I was pretty young.
So yes, the family would do a lot of the things like I described.
Like when we went skiing and we went to these vacations, the family was very-- it seemed to me we would often go together.
You know, rent a villa for the family rather than just for our parents and my sister and me.
There would be like, you know, 12 people there, 14 people, and-- and so forth.
But in that kind of a world, you had no sense of anti-Semitism?
Did you even understand that Jews were different than the Catholics?
Or how did that--
No--
--I didn't, because I was protected from it.
This-- you know, we were not living in the ghetto.

I remember on one or two occasions-- maybe there were more, I don't remember that-- but I remember going to visit poorer relatives who lived either in the ghetto or who lived and dressed the way one associates with Orthodox Jews prior to World War II.
My grandfather had a beard and looked like that, but it was still within the context of a wealthy family.
And I remember feeling the difference, like going into their apartment and could smell the cooking.
It was kind of cramped and closed.
Living with my mother's family.
My father's family came from Lwow, Lemberg, I believe it's also called.
I knew about them.
They had an interesting history.
In fact, some of his brothers had come to America in the 1920s and actually worked briefly in films, which is interesting because here I am today living in Los Angeles [LAUGHS] and working in films.
They all went back after making some money and they were all killed by Hitler.
So I didn't, I didn't-- I don't, you know, I don't remember--
No.
Just stories about them.
In the Lax family, yes.
Yes, my mother's family.
Yeah, I do.
I do have some memories, but they're, again, a very young child's memory.
I'm five years old or six years old.
I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being brought into room, probably to say goodnight to my parents.
I think that was the, you know, the way it was done-- especially if there was company.
Where we would be put in our pajamas and then we would come in and, you know, say good night.

And I remember coming in the room, and my parents and some of my uncles and aunts, sitting around listening to shortwave radio and hearing this rather angry or strange-sounding German voice-- which I only realized in retrospect later-- years later when I discussed it with my sister and my mother-- was Hitler.
So they were listening to Hitler giving one of his speeches that was being broadcast.
And there was definitely a feeling of tension in the room.
I don't remember ongoing discussions about it, but I do remember that.
I know there were family discussions.
I don't remember whether I ever had any sense of those directly or because I know about them because I was told about them later on-- those two things tend to fuse, you know, together.
You don't know what's recovered memory and what's just remembered anecdotes.

But I do know that what happened was that when the war was coming, we were in a situation of, first of all-- I mean, I'm not saying 'we' because my sister and I were way too young to have any political sense, but the adults in the family.
There was definitely a sense that war, you know, that war was imminent.
But at the same time, there was that belief that it could not happen because England would not-- was-- had a, had a some kind of a pact with Poland.
That if Poland was invaded, England would go to war.
So the belief was, there was no way that Germany would risk starting a war with England in order to attack Poland.
That was the general belief.
That was one aspect of it.
I think what happened-- and this again, I'm sort of piecing together from what I've heard and-- but basically, it was something like this.

The family-- or the bulk of the family-- was on vacation-- another one of those many vacations-- when-- in fact, Zakopane, the place I mentioned before where the skiing, except this was summer or spring.
You could also go to a beautiful place in the mountains.
We were there on vacation and the war began.
And--
Yeah.
And the-- we, we got information or letters or communications-- I don't know how that worked-- that it would be advisable not to go back to Cracow.
That it was dangerous to go back there.

One thing that happened was that as soon as the Germans came into Poland-- to that part of Poland-- there was a reward out for my grandfather because he represented one of the several dozen very prominent, wealthy Jews that they wanted to get their hands on.
So the decision was made not to take chances and not to go back to Cracow.
I think the thing evolved slowly from there-- how we were not going to go back.
Zakopane.
I was there with my parents, my sister, the governess-- you know, the entourage.
And aunts and uncles and, you know, the other members of the family.
There was a discussion going on in the family.
My-- I think for some time prior to the Germans actually invading, there was no, there was no, there was no real agreement in the-- total agreement-- in the family as what to do.
I know that my mother and one of my uncles were arguing, in fact, strongly for getting out.
And the others were saying, go where?
And give this up?
And it'll never happen.
That was sort of the thing.
So once the war started, the decision was made, no, we're not going back.
How it was reached-- you know, what the counsels were-- that, I don't know.
So what did they say to you?
I mean, you're this five-year-old.
And what'd they say?
You didn't say much to-- that generation, people didn't say much to five-year-olds.
They just, you just did it.
Do you know what I mean?
There was not-- it's not like today where things were discussed with children and their feelings were taken into consideration.
It was more like, we're going here.
We're going there.
We're gonna go to this other place right now and everything's going to be OK.
And we ended up going to-- to get away from that part of Poland, we went-- started to move towards the Russian border, which was Chelm.
Those stories-- It was a Polish town with a very large Jewish ghetto.
And we had some distant relatives there, and we went to stay with them.
I don't remember the trip there.
I do remember the tremendous difference of living, suddenly, in cramped quarters now with these people, which was so different than the life I, we had lived in Cracow.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember, strangely enough, my-- that's, that was the time when I began to experience feelings of anxiety because I think as a child-- and I think I was pretty sensitive that way anyway-- that I was beginning to pick up the vibes of what the war was about.
And what they fused with were ghost stories that a cousin of ours used to tell us.
And-- you know, about witches and goblins and this and that.
I, I was terribly, terribly affected by those stories.
Frightened me probably more than any of my other cousins and sister.
I mean, I really-- it would stay with me well into the night.
So I think I was picking up the--
--the war.
Governess went with us to Chelm.
It was-- I believe it was there that the decision was finally made that she would not be able to continue on with us.
It was a money question.
It was also a question of, you know, not-- just not being able to keep her anymore, plus the fact that we were going to try to get out of the country.
So--
This was essentially my, my parents, my sister and I, plus my uncles and aunts.
Altogether, there were about 14 or 15 of us, including my grandparents.
The youngest of us were my cousin and I were about six.
The oldest were my grandparents, who were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in their 70s, I guess.
It's quite a wide range.
While we were in Chelm, I remember the Polish army retreating.
I definitely remember that.
That was very-- the colors.
They used to have these funny kind of pea green uniforms.
They have practically no mechanized-- it was all horses and wagons.
It was very primitive.
But I remember them withdrawing.
There were a couple of sort of mini air raids.
I remember one time, we-- there was like an improvised air raid shelter behind the house.
And it seemed that the air raid was over and we went out-- or I went out of the shelter-- and the next thing I knew, there was a German plane overhead.
And somebody grabbed me and pulled me back down into the shelter.
I, I, I remember that.
I remember my sister and my cousin, who were the oldest of us children, finding-- this was after the Germans had already come in, because they did come into, into Chelm-- finding a pistol.
A German Luger.
And it was-- which we thought was fantastic.
Of course, if you were found with a gun, everyone was instantly killed.
And I didn't know that.
By the Germans, yeah.
By the Germans.
And I remember my sister and my cousin taking the pistol somewhere in a field and burying it and getting rid of it.
So they had enough sense of that.
The governess leaves at some point here.
I don't remember exactly when it is.
I do know that when she leaves, somewhere around then, and I know it's connected with it, I get very, very sick.
I develop a terrible ear infection.
And now, there are no doctors to be had.
There's no drugs to be had.
There are curfews at night.
The Gestapo has already been to the house.
They are beginning to sort of, I guess, figure out who's Jewish and who isn't.
You know, what they're going to do with it.
German soldiers have been coming into the house to requisition things.

One time, they come into the house and my grandmother takes some zloty-- you know, Polish currency-- maybe the equivalent of $5,000 or $10,000 that she had taken with her, and wrapping it up in rags-- wet rags-- and putting it into the stove so they won't look there and won't find it.
And they don't find it.
They leave and she opens the stove and the whole thing is burnt to a crisp.
[LAUGHS] It's that kind of a time.
Anyway, the governess leaves, and I'm very sick.
There's-- so the family at this point splits up into smaller groups.

We know we're not going to be able to get out of there in one group, and so the men go-- three or four of the men-- go in one group, some of the aunts and uncles with their children go in another group.
Finally, when I'm better, we leave Chelm with-- that's my, my aunt, my cousin who's my age, and my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember two things from that exodus.
One is that we were stopped at some point by-- at a German-- by German soldiers at a checkpoint.
No, we're in a wagon.
We're being ferried out of there-- ferried sounds like on a boat, but I mean we're being taken out of there in like one of those wagons with a-- hay wagon.
We're paying for that.
It's a kind of Underground Railroad that's being run by Jews, I guess, and non-Jews.
This case, I think the guy was Jewish who was taking us out.
My mother has the good sense, when the guards come over-- the German guards-- to talk to her and my aunt, to instruct my aunt to act like she doesn't understand a word they are saying.

Because both my aunt and my mother speak a perfect German and there is the realization on her part-- which was rather shrewd-- that if they act like they speak or understand German, the German guards might then say, well, God, they understand German because they're Jewish and they speak Yiddish.
Since Yiddish and German are connected languages.
So they act like they don't understand them at all.
My mother actually has refused to part with and has with her a little prayer book which she has hidden back there in that hay wagon.
So which, if that was found, of course the jig would be up.
We--
Whatever we were taking was, was very little.
And some of it probably had gone ahead of us because we were, you know, like I said, we didn't all travel together carrying the same things.
We have some chocolate bars that are still left from Suchard.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember that.
Also, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm trying to think if there was anything else specifically.

No, but I do remember that on that journey out of Chelm, somebody comes riding up to our wagon-- coming from Chelm up to where we are-- to tell the guy who is, who-- who's Jewish, who was taking us out of there, that the Germans are beginning to round up the Jewish population.
They're making their first steps to round them up, I guess, to put them in a controlled ghetto or wherever they're going to put them.
And this man has to make a choice as to whether he's gonna take us to the destination he's promised to take us to or whether he's just going to say, sorry folks, I got to go back.
And he takes us.
And that's-- the whole journey was like that.
Very existential.
In any moment, you could be discovered.
You could be caught.
A guy could leave you in the lurch.
You know.
Yes.
All of them killed later by the Germans.
I think in, I think in a place like Chelm, probably 98% of the Jews who lived there were exterminated by the Nazis later on.
Yes.
We go very briefly to Lwow-- to my father's hometown.
I'm not sure why we go there, but I do know that his side of the family represent those people who I said before who decide that they're going to stay put.
That they are, they're not going to leave.
That they-- and so they, they remain.
And we continue on, and we go to Vilno-- Vilnius, as the Lithuanians call it.
The capital of Lithuania.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What has been happening is that Poland and Lithuania are being divided in the seesaw between Russia and Germany, who-- at that point, Hitler and Stalin have a pact.
So even while we were in Poland, in Chelm, my, my memory is that the Poles evacuated-- or the army withdrew.
The Germans come in briefly, then the Russians come in briefly-- not because they are fighting each other, but because they take their territory.
Then the Germans come back to stay.
At this point, Lithuania is under Russian control, and therefore is not-- doesn't pose the same immediate danger that being under the Germans does.
Right.
Do you have any idea how they had done their money?
What they did-- how they had traveling--?
Not, not really.
My impression is that what happened was they took some jewelry with them.
Probably as much money as they could get their hands on quickly.
I think a lot of stuff had been frozen by the Polish government once the war began, so you couldn't take stuff out.
So I don't think it's like they've been able to take vast amounts, but I think enough, you know, for the bribes or to get-- buy-- that ride on the hay truck.
You know, I mean the wagon that will get you out of there.
And to get to Lithuania-- to make the journey-- I do remember, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tremendous amount of traveling at night.
And sleds.
This is with Poles.
Which month-- what month were we at now?
Well by this time, we must be obviously in winter.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] You know, the war has started when?
In the summer of that year, or late summer, or August--
--or something?
This must be the winter of that year.

I remember it being so cold at night that my parents make my sister and me stand on the runners of the sled from time to time-- on the back-- because there's the fear that if we fall asleep, we may not wake up.
